title: Heegaard Floer homology and alternating knots
overall_summary: Ozsváth and Szabó give a combinatorial description of the generators, filtration levels, and absolute gradings of the knot Floer complex associated to a decorated knot projection, using Kauffman states. For alternating knots and non-split alternating links, this description collapses the homology onto a single grading line determined by the Alexander polynomial and signature. The central combinatorial mechanism is the checkerboard or Tait graph dictionary: Kauffman states correspond to spanning trees of the black graph, and global equalities are proved by checking elementary transposition moves between states. For the original weighted-tree lattice problem, the paper is most useful as a source of graph-state encodings, local-to-global arguments via connected move graphs, canonical state constructions by finite exhaustion, and the quoted definite-intersection-form constraints involving characteristic vectors and correction terms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Kauffman state/spanning-tree correspondence, Section 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be given by a decorated planar projection with four-valent projection graph G, distinguished edge, and distinguished adjacent complementary regions A and B, with A chosen to be the unbounded region. A Kauffman state assigns to each crossing of G one of its four incident quadrants, such that distinct crossings are assigned quadrants lying in distinct complementary regions and no assigned quadrant lies in A or B. Chessboard-color the complementary regions of S^2 − G, and form the black graph whose vertices are the black regions and whose edges are the crossings of G, joining the two opposite black regions incident at that crossing. Then Kauffman states are in one-to-one correspondence with maximal subtrees, i.e. spanning trees, of the black graph: a state x is sent to the set of crossings at which x chooses a black quadrant, viewed as edges of the black graph.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as a simple graph-theoretic description. A Kauffman state gives a bijection between the crossings of G and the non-distinguished complementary regions S^2 − G − A − B. In particular, among the black regions, every black region except the distinguished black one receives exactly one marker. Thus the crossings at which the marker is black give exactly one fewer edge than the number of black vertices. Planar duality with the white graph shows that these selected black edges cannot contain a cycle and cannot fail to span: a black cycle would separate white regions in a way incompatible with the distinct-region condition for the complementary white markers, and disconnectedness is dual to a white cycle. Conversely, a spanning tree of the black graph determines black markers on its edges and the dual complement determines the white markers, giving a unique Kauffman state.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2, Kauffman's Clock Theorem
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a fixed decorated knot projection, any two distinct Kauffman states x and y can be connected by a finite sequence of transpositions. Here two states differ by a transposition if there are two crossings v1 and v2 and a straight path P along the knot projection from v1 to v2, not containing the distinguished edge, such that x and y agree at every crossing except v1 and v2, and at v1 and v2 the two states switch the two quadrant choices adjacent to the first and last edges of P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is not reproduced in this paper; it is cited from Kauffman's Formal Knot Theory, Chapter 2. The paper uses the theorem as a connectivity statement for the graph of Kauffman states. Once a quantity is shown to transform in the desired way under a single transposition, the Clock Theorem promotes the local verification to all states.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2, Kauffman-state model for the classical knot Floer data
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be an oriented knot in S^3, and choose a decorated planar projection of K. For each crossing v, let ε(v) be the sign of the crossing. The orientation of K singles out at v a quadrant pointed towards v, whose boundary contains the two incoming edges, and the opposite quadrant pointed away from v, whose boundary contains the two outgoing edges. For a Kauffman state x, define a local filtration contribution s(x,v) by 2 ε(v) s(x,v)=1 if x(v) is the pointed-towards quadrant, 2 ε(v) s(x,v)=−1 if x(v) is the pointed-away quadrant, and s(x,v)=0 otherwise. Define S(x)=sum_v s(x,v). Define a local grading contribution m(x,v) by m(x,v)=−ε(v) if x(v) is the pointed-away quadrant and m(x,v)=0 otherwise, and set M(x)=sum_v m(x,v). Then there is a Heegaard diagram for K such that the knot Floer complex widehat CFK(S^3,K) is freely generated by the Kauffman states of the decorated projection. If a generator corresponds to a state x, its filtration level is F(x)=S(x), and its absolute grading is gr(x)=M(x).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The diagram is built directly from the projection graph G. Let Σ be the boundary of a regular neighborhood of G in S^3, oriented as the boundary of the complement. For every complementary region r except the unbounded region A, place an α-curve α_r following the boundary of r. For every crossing v, place a β-curve β_v locally as in Figure 4 of the paper. Add a meridian μ supported near the distinguished edge. The meridian meets only the α-curve corresponding to the other distinguished region B, so an intersection point in T_α ∩ T_β chooses, for every crossing β_v, one incident region other than A or B, with no region repeated. Thus generators are exactly Kauffman states. For the filtration, Lemma 2.3 proves that if two states differ by a transposition, then the difference of their Floer filtration levels equals the difference of the combinatorial sums S. By the Clock Theorem this gives S(x)=F(x)+c for a constant c independent of x. The constant is zero because the Kauffman state sum sum_x (±1) T^{S(x)} is the Conway-normalized Alexander polynomial, while the Euler characteristic of knot Floer homology gives the symmetrized Alexander polynomial with the normalization used to define F. For the absolute grading, Lemma 2.4 gives the corresponding equality of grading differences under any transposition, so M(x)=gr(x)+c' for a constant c'. Lemma 2.5 constructs a canonical state x0 for which all local terms m(x0,v) vanish. Sliding the β-curves forward across the reference point z but never across w gives a Heegaard diagram for S^3 with a unique intersection point corresponding to x0; that unique generator has absolute degree zero. Hence c'=0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3, filtration change under a transposition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let x and y be Kauffman states of a decorated knot projection which differ by a transposition, and let the same letters denote the corresponding generators of the knot Floer complex in the Heegaard diagram constructed from the projection. Then F(x) − F(y) = S(x) − S(y), where F is the knot Floer filtration and S is the combinatorial filtration sum from Theorem 1.2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Orient the edge path P involved in the transposition according to the knot orientation. There are four cases, according to whether the path leaves or enters under-crossings or over-crossings at its two endpoints. In the case where the path goes from an under-crossing to an over-crossing, after possibly switching x and y one constructs a Whitney disk class φ from x to y whose domain has local multiplicities zero or one, with n_w(φ)=0 and n_z(φ)=1. The domain is a connected surface with boundary components coming from the meridian, from β-circles for intermediate vertices, and from the α-curves surrounding intermediate regions; its detailed topology is irrelevant for the filtration calculation. Thus F(x)−F(y)=n_z(φ)−n_w(φ)=1, and a direct local check of the four quadrants gives S(x)−S(y)=1. If both endpoints are under-crossings, there is a square domain, possibly with holes from intermediate crossings, disjoint from both basepoints w and z, so n_w=n_z=0 and both filtration differences vanish. The two remaining over/under endpoint cases are handled analogously.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.4, absolute grading change under a transposition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let x and y be Kauffman states which differ by a transposition. For the ordered pair for which the positive transposition domain is a Whitney disk class φ from x to y with n_w(φ)=0, the absolute gradings satisfy gr(x) − gr(y)=1. In particular, a transposition changes absolute grading by exactly one in absolute value, with the sign determined by the orientation of the transposition domain.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The domains constructed for transpositions in Lemma 2.3 are topologically squares with finitely many handles attached and finitely many disks removed. Writing the corresponding intersection points as x={x1,...,xg} and y={y1,...,yg}, only two coordinates change; the remaining coordinates either lie in the handles, on the circular boundary components, or outside the domain. Coordinates in handles can be removed by destabilizing the Heegaard diagram, which does not change the Maslov index relevant to this disk. After these deletions the computation reduces to a Whitney disk whose domain is a square with finitely many disks removed. The paper computes the Maslov index of such a class to be μ(φ)=1 by comparing with an explicit Heegaard diagram for a connected sum of S^1 × S^2's, where the two generators are known to differ in grading by one. Since n_w(φ)=0 and gr(x)−gr(y)=μ(φ)−2 n_w(φ), the grading difference is one.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.5, existence and uniqueness of the canonical state
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a decorated oriented knot projection. Order the edges by traversing the knot from the distinguished edge. For a crossing v, let the edge after v be the last edge in this traversal whose closure contains v, and let the v-penultimate edge e_v be the edge immediately preceding it. Then there is a unique Kauffman state x0 such that, for every crossing v, the quadrant x0(v) is one of the two quadrants whose closure contains e_v.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let X0=A ∪ B be the union of the two distinguished regions. The proof inductively exhausts S^2 by regions X0 ⊂ X1 ⊂ ... ⊂ Xm=S^2 while defining x0 on crossings v1,...,vn. The induction maintains that x0 maps the chosen crossings bijectively to the regions in Xn − G − X0 and that each previously chosen v_i-penultimate edge lies in the interior of Xn. If Xn is not all of S^2, then because the graph is a knot projection, Xn has corners. Choose the last corner v_{n+1} with respect to the traversal order. Its penultimate edge has one adjacent quadrant in Xn and one adjacent quadrant in a new outside region R_{n+1}; otherwise there would be a later corner. This crossing was not previously chosen, since all earlier penultimate edges lie in the interior of Xn. Define x0(v_{n+1}) to be the outside quadrant in R_{n+1}, and set X_{n+1}=Xn ∪ R_{n+1}. At every step there is no choice, so the resulting state exists and is unique.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1, Gordon-Litherland signature formula for alternating projections
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a knot with an alternating projection G. Color the complementary regions in a chessboard pattern so that the under-first quadrants at crossings are white and the over-first quadrants are black, where a quadrant is over-first if, when its boundary is oriented as part of the plane, the first of the two crossing edges is the over-strand. With the paper's convention that the left-handed trefoil has signature +2, the signature of K is σ(K)=number of black regions in the planar projection − number of positive crossings − 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is not given in this paper; the result is cited as Theorem 6 of Gordon-Litherland and as interpreted by Lee. Ozsváth and Szabó use it as the bridge between the local Kauffman-state grading calculation and the classical signature. In the proof of Theorem 1.3, after summing local contributions over all crossings, the expression obtained is exactly the right-hand side of this formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.3, knot Floer homology of alternating knots
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K ⊂ S^3 be an alternating knot. Write its symmetrized Alexander polynomial as ∆_K(T)=a_0 + sum_{s>0} a_s (T^s + T^{−s}). Let σ(K) be the knot signature with the convention that the left-handed trefoil has signature +2. Then for every integer s, widehat HFK(S^3,K,s) is supported entirely in absolute grading s + σ(K)/2, and widehat HFK(S^3,K,s) is isomorphic to Z^{|a_s|}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose an alternating decorated projection and use the Heegaard diagram from Theorem 1.2. A local inspection of the four possible quadrants at each crossing relates m(x,v)−s(x,v), the crossing sign, and whether the chosen quadrant is under-first or over-first. In an alternating diagram all over-first quadrants have the same checkerboard color, chosen black. Since a Kauffman state represents every black region except the distinguished black region exactly once, summing the local identity gives 2(M(x)−S(x)) = number of black regions − number of positive crossings − 1. By Theorem 3.1, this equals σ(K), hence every generator in filtration level S=s has absolute grading M=s+σ(K)/2. Therefore the differential in each fixed filtration level must vanish for grading reasons. The Euler characteristic of widehat HFK in filtration s is the coefficient a_s of the Alexander polynomial, so the rank of the group is |a_s| and it lies in the stated grading.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Skein exact sequence for link Floer homology, used in Section 4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Suppose L_+ is an oriented link projection with a positive crossing p whose two strands belong to the same component of L_+. Let L_- be obtained by changing p to a negative crossing, and let L_0 be the oriented resolution at p. Then for each filtration level s there is a long exact sequence ... → widehat HFK(L_-,s) → widehat HFK(L_0,s) → widehat HFK(L_+,s) → ... . In the grading convention used in the paper, the first two maps mentioned drop absolute grading by 1/2, and the remaining map is non-increasing in absolute grading.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This exact sequence is recalled from the authors' earlier work on link Floer homology and is not proved in this paper. In Section 4 it is combined with the alternating-knot calculation and signature shifts under local skein modifications to induct on the number of link components.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1, link Floer homology of non-split alternating links
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L ⊂ S^3 be a non-split oriented alternating link with n components, Alexander-Conway polynomial ∆_L, and signature σ(L). Write (T^{−1/2} − T^{1/2})^{n−1} ∆_L(T) = a_0 + sum_{s>0} a_s (T^s + T^{−s}). Then for every integer s, widehat HFK(S^3,L,s) is supported entirely in absolute grading s + σ(L)/2, and widehat HFK(S^3,L,s) is isomorphic to Z^{|a_s|}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on the number of components. The base case is Theorem 1.3 for knots. For the inductive step, choose a crossing p where two different strands of L meet. The paper constructs two alternating links L_- and L_+ with one additional crossing q whose oriented resolution at q is L. According to the sign of p, the changes alter either the number of positive crossings or the number of black regions in a controlled way. Applying the Gordon-Litherland signature formula gives σ(L_-)−1=σ(L)=σ(L_+)+1. The skein exact sequence then relates the groups of L_-, L, and L_+. By the induction hypothesis, the groups for L_- and L_+ lie on grading lines shifted by exactly the amounts dictated by the maps in the exact sequence. Exactness forces the group for L to lie on the line s+σ(L)/2. Finally, the Euler characteristic calculation for link Floer homology identifies the rank with the absolute value of the coefficient a_s.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Quoted correction-term inequality and Elkies lattice theorem, Section 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper recalls two external definite-lattice constraints. First, from Ozsváth-Szabó's absolutely graded Floer theory: if Y is an integer homology three-sphere, W is a smooth negative-definite four-manifold with boundary Y, and ξ is any characteristic vector for the intersection form on H_2(W;Z), meaning Q(ξ,v) ≡ Q(v,v) mod 2 for every v, then ξ^2 + rk H_2(W;Z) ≤ 4 d(Y), where d(Y) is the Heegaard Floer correction term. Second, from Elkies: for any negative-definite unimodular integral lattice V, max over characteristic vectors ξ of ξ^2 + rk(V) is at least 0, with equality if and only if V is diagonalizable over Z.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Neither result is proved in this paper. They are quoted to explain the role of correction terms as obstructions to exotic definite intersection forms bounding a fixed three-manifold. The inequality bounds every characteristic vector in a negative-definite bounding form by 4d(Y), while Elkies' theorem says that, for unimodular negative-definite forms, the maximum characteristic-vector defect ξ^2+rk(V) detects diagonalizability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1.5, correction term of +1 surgery on an alternating knot
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K ⊂ S^3 be an alternating knot. Then the Heegaard Floer correction term of +1 surgery on K is d(S^3_1(K)) = 2 min(0, −ceil(−σ(K)/4)), where σ(K) is the signature in the paper's convention.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper uses the general relation d(S^3_1(K)) = d_{1/2}(S^3_0(K)) − 1/2. When σ(K) ≤ 0, Theorem 1.4 gives the relevant d_{1/2} term for zero-surgery directly. When σ(K)>0, the knot is reflected: if r(K) is the reflection, then d_{1/2}(S^3_0(K)) = −d_{−1/2}(S^3_0(r(K))) = 1/2, again using Theorem 1.4 for the reflected knot. Substituting gives the displayed closed formula.

proof_techniques.name: Checkerboard graph encoding by Kauffman states
proof_techniques.description: The paper converts a decorated planar knot projection into a black Tait graph whose vertices are black regions and whose edges are crossings. Kauffman states, originally assignments of quadrants to crossings avoiding two distinguished regions, become spanning trees of this graph. This replaces a planar local-choice problem by a finite graph-theoretic object with a root or distinguished region.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original object is already a finite tree with a lattice form attached. The Tait-graph viewpoint suggests rooting or distinguishing a vertex or region and translating lattice questions into combinatorics of subtrees, leaves, and local choices.

proof_techniques.name: Local-to-global reduction via transposition connectivity
proof_techniques.description: The Clock Theorem says the space of Kauffman states is connected under elementary transpositions supported along a path between two crossings. Ozsváth and Szabó prove identities for filtration and grading by checking the change under a single transposition, then use connectivity to conclude that the identities hold for all states up to a constant, which is fixed by normalization.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a tree lattice, one can try to define a graph of possible decompositions, sign assignments, or support choices and prove it is connected under elementary local moves. If irreducibility or reducibility changes can be controlled locally along an edge or path, a global contradiction may follow from a single normalized case.

proof_techniques.name: Canonical state by finite exhaustion
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 2.5 constructs a unique canonical Kauffman state by starting from the two distinguished regions and repeatedly attaching an outside region at the last available corner. The proof is a deterministic induction on finitely many regions, maintaining that previously chosen penultimate edges lie in the interior of the exhausted set.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a useful template for proofs on finite trees: start from a distinguished location, peel off or attach one extremal piece at a time, and maintain a local invariant. The hypothesis that exactly one vertex is bad may be suited to a similar rooted pruning argument.

proof_techniques.name: Reduction of global domains to standard local pieces
proof_techniques.description: In Lemma 2.4, transposition domains may have handles and holes, but the Maslov-index calculation ignores irrelevant handles by destabilization and reduces to a square with disks removed. The essential numerical effect of a complicated global move is therefore computed from a standard local model.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a lattice decomposition u=a+b, the support of a and b in a tree may have extraneous branches. A parallel strategy would reduce a putative reducibility configuration to a minimal connected support, often a path or local star, where dot products can be computed explicitly.

proof_techniques.name: Summing local contributions to obtain a global invariant
proof_techniques.description: The alternating-knot calculation assigns local quantities s(x,v) and m(x,v) at each crossing. A local identity relating these quantities to crossing signs and checkerboard colors is summed over all crossings; because a state marks every black region except one, the sum becomes a global count of black regions and positive crossings, then the Gordon-Litherland formula identifies it with the signature.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The tree-lattice condition w(v)<d(v) is a local imbalance between weight and valence. A proof might assign local surplus or deficit terms to vertices and sum them over a support subtree to force a vertex satisfying the desired irreducibility property.

proof_techniques.name: Definite-form constraints through characteristic vectors
proof_techniques.description: The paper recalls that Heegaard Floer correction terms bound characteristic vectors in negative-definite four-manifold intersection forms, and pairs this with Elkies' theorem for unimodular definite lattices. This is a standard way to convert topology of a boundary three-manifold into arithmetic restrictions on a definite lattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: After reversing sign, the positive-definite tree lattice in the original problem can be viewed as a negative-definite graph lattice. Characteristic-vector inequalities or Elkies-type extremal arguments may help force structural constraints, such as the existence of a basis vertex that cannot split with nonnegative pairing.

other_useful_info: The paper itself does not formulate graph-lattice irreducibility, but it does supply several adjacent tools and references. Kauffman [16] is cited for the Clock Theorem, and Kauffman [17] for the Alexander polynomial state-sum normalization. Gordon-Litherland [15] and Lee [19] are cited for the signature formula of alternating diagrams, which is the likely source for checkerboard-surface intersection forms. Ozsváth-Szabó [28] is cited for correction-term inequalities for definite four-manifolds with boundary, and Elkies [8] for the characteristic-vector characterization of diagonal definite unimodular lattices.
why_relevant: Uses Goeritz matrices of alternating diagrams as definite graph lattices and relates them to sharp four-manifolds. It is a standard source for the definite-lattice viewpoint behind many later graph-lattice irreducibility arguments.
